Factory](/wiki/Shout!_Factory "Shout! Factory") * [Thirty Tigers](/wiki/Thirty_Tigers "Thirty Tigers")

is an American [Western swing music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") group that was formed in [Paw Paw, West Virginia](/wiki/Paw_Paw,_West_Virginia "Paw Paw, West Virginia"), in 1970,[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) and is based in [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"). The band has won nine Grammy Awards, released over 20 albums, appeared in multiple films, toured widely in America and Europe, and charted more than 21 singles on the *Billboard* country charts. Their highest-charting single, "[The Letter That Johnny Walker Read](/wiki/The_Letter_That_Johnny_Walker_Read "The Letter That Johnny Walker Read")", peaked at number 10 in 1975.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American band

This article is about the band. For the Bloodhound Gang song, see [One Fierce Beer Coaster](/wiki/One_Fierce_Beer_Coaster "One Fierce Beer Coaster").

**Asleep at the Wheel** is an American [Western swing music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") group that was formed in [Paw Paw, West Virginia](/wiki/Paw_Paw,_West_Virginia "Paw Paw, West Virginia"), in 1970,[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) and is based in [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"). The band has won nine Grammy Awards, released over 20 albums, appeared in multiple films, toured widely in America and Europe, and charted more than 21 singles on the *Billboard* country charts. Their highest-charting single, "[The Letter That Johnny Walker Read](/wiki/The_Letter_That_Johnny_Walker_Read "The Letter That Johnny Walker Read")", peaked at number 10 in 1975.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asleep_at_the_Wheel&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Beginnings to Austin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asleep_at_the_Wheel&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginnings to Austin")]

In 1969, [Ray Benson](/wiki/Ray_Benson "Ray Benson") and [Lucky Oceans](/wiki/Lucky_Oceans "Lucky Oceans") (Reuben Gosfield), both natives of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, co-founded Asleep at the Wheel in Paw Paw, West Virginia, and soon after they found themselves opening for [Alice Cooper](/wiki/Alice_Cooper "Alice Cooper") and [Hot Tuna](/wiki/Hot_Tuna "Hot Tuna") in [Washington, DC](/wiki/Washington,_DC "Washington, DC").[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) A year later, they moved to [East Oakland, California](/wiki/East_Oakland,_California "East Oakland, California"), at the invitation of [Commander Cody and His Lost Planet Airmen](/wiki/Commander_Cody_and_His_Lost_Planet_Airmen "Commander Cody and His Lost Planet Airmen").[[5]](#cite_note-allmusicguide-5) After being mentioned in *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine by [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison"), they landed a record deal with [United Artists](/wiki/United_Artists "United Artists").[[5]](#cite_note-allmusicguide-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1973, their debut album, *[Comin' Right at Ya](/wiki/Comin%27_Right_at_Ya "Comin' Right at Ya")*, was released by United Artists. At the invitation of [Willie Nelson](/wiki/Willie_Nelson "Willie Nelson"), they left Oakland for Austin in 1974.[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4)

### 1974–1979

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asleep_at_the_Wheel&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1974–1979")]

In 1974, Asleep at the Wheel released its [self-titled second album](/wiki/Asleep_at_the_Wheel_(album) "Asleep at the Wheel (album)"), with a cover of [Louis Jordan](/wiki/Louis_Jordan "Louis Jordan")'s "[Choo Choo Ch'Boogie](/wiki/Choo_Choo_Ch%27Boogie "Choo Choo Ch'Boogie")", which was the band's first single to hit the country charts. The following year had the release of *[Texas Gold](/wiki/Texas_Gold "Texas Gold")*, with the top-10 country hit single "The Letter That Johnny Walker Read". In addition, they played on [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS")'s *[Austin City Limits](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits "Austin City Limits")*, where they have since performed a record-setting 10 times.[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) In 1977, the band was voted Best Country Western Band by *Rolling Stone* and was awarded the Touring Band of the Year by the [Academy of Country Music](/wiki/Academy_of_Country_Music "Academy of Country Music"). They also went on tour with [Emmylou Harris](/wiki/Emmylou_Harris "Emmylou Harris") in Europe.[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) The following year, they recorded a cover of [Count Basie](/wiki/Count_Basie "Count Basie")'s song "[One O'Clock Jump](/wiki/One_O%27Clock_Jump "One O'Clock Jump")".[[5]](#cite_note-allmusicguide-5) Also in 1980, they appeared in the movie *[Roadie](/wiki/Roadie_(1980_film) "Roadie (1980 film)")*, along with [Meat Loaf](/wiki/Meat_Loaf "Meat Loaf"), [Blondie](/wiki/Blondie_(band) "Blondie (band)"), and [Art Carney](/wiki/Art_Carney "Art Carney"). By the end of the decade, the band recorded their first live album, *Served Live*, at the [Austin Opera House](/w/index.php?title=Austin_Opera_House&action=edit&redlink=1 "Austin Opera House (page does not exist)").[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4)

### 1980–1989

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asleep_at_the_Wheel&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980–1989")]

The 1980s became a turbulent decade for the band. After moving to [MCA](/wiki/MCA_Records "MCA Records"), co-founder Lucky Oceans left, followed in September 1986 by Chris O'Connell due to her pregnancy. Asleep at the Wheel gathered a large amount of debt that required it to work on [commercials](/wiki/Television_advertisement "Television advertisement") and [movie soundtracks](/wiki/Movie_soundtrack "Movie soundtrack"). This band produced the soundtrack for the film *[Liar's Moon](/wiki/Liar%27s_Moon "Liar's Moon")*.

In 1985, the band released a virtually ignored self-titled album.[[5]](#cite_note-allmusicguide-5) By the late 1980s, Ray Benson had done some producing, allowing the band a second chance with [Epic Records](/wiki/Epic_Records "Epic Records"). In 1987, the band released *10*, which won them their second Grammy for Best Country Instrumental, helping to launch their comeback. The album also had contributions from legendary fiddle player and onetime [Texas Playboys](/wiki/Texas_Playboys "Texas Playboys") member [Johnny Gimble](/wiki/Johnny_Gimble "Johnny Gimble").[[5]](#cite_note-allmusicguide-5) The following year, the band released *Western Standard Time*, which won them another Grammy for Best Country Instrumental.[[5]](#cite_note-allmusicguide-5) The late 1980s also had the growth of [Jann Browne](/wiki/Jann_Browne "Jann Browne") as a solo vocalist in the group; in 1989 she would later embark on a solo career on [Curb Records](/wiki/Curb_Records "Curb Records").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### 1990–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asleep_at_the_Wheel&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990–1999")]

The band moved to [Arista Records](/wiki/Arista_Records "Arista Records") and released the album, *Keepin' Me Up Nights*. Soon after, the band had turnover. Among its new members was former solo singer [Rosie Flores](/wiki/Rosie_Flores "Rosie Flores"), who joined in 1997.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 1991, Ray Benson directed the music[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) and co-starred in the movie *[Wild Texas Wind](/wiki/Wild_Texas_Wind "Wild Texas Wind")* with [Dolly Parton](/wiki/Dolly_Parton "Dolly Parton").[[9]](#cite_note-IMDB-9) In honor of the 66th anniversary of [Route 66](/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66"), the band launched the *Route 66 Tour*.[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) In 1993, the band released an instant hit with several guest musicians,[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) *A Tribute to the Music of Bob Wills and the Texas Playboys*, to much critical acclaim.[[5]](#cite_note-allmusicguide-5) Two years later, the band celebrated their 25th anniversary[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) by releasing *The Wheel Keeps on Rollin*.[[5]](#cite_note-allmusicguide-5) In 1999, the band and [DreamWorks](/wiki/DreamWorks_Pictures "DreamWorks Pictures") released *Ride with Bob*, as their second tribute album to [Bob Wills](/wiki/Bob_Wills "Bob Wills"). This album also enjoyed immediate success and garnered the band two Grammy wins, one for Best Country Instrumental,[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) and the other for Best Package Design.

According to *Rolling Stone* magazine, Ray Benson "didn't just enlist the obvious Wills fans" in this tribute album to Bob Wills like [Merle Haggard](/wiki/Merle_Haggard "Merle Haggard"), [Willie Nelson](/wiki/Willie_Nelson "Willie Nelson"), and [Lyle Lovett](/wiki/Lyle_Lovett "Lyle Lovett"). He also brought in some of country's young lions the [Dixie Chicks](/wiki/Dixie_Chicks "Dixie Chicks"), [Tim McGraw](/wiki/Tim_McGraw "Tim McGraw"), and [Lee Ann Womack](/wiki/Lee_Ann_Womack "Lee Ann Womack"), some [pop](/wiki/Pop_(music) "Pop (music)") stars", including [Shawn Colvin](/wiki/Shawn_Colvin "Shawn Colvin"), the [Squirrel Nut Zippers](/wiki/Squirrel_Nut_Zippers "Squirrel Nut Zippers"), and fellow country preservationists such as [Dwight Yoakam](/wiki/Dwight_Yoakam "Dwight Yoakam").[[10]](#cite_note-Dansby-10)

### 2000–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asleep_at_the_Wheel&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000–present")]

In 2000, the Dixie Chicks were nominated for an award for Vocal Event of the Year for "[Roly Poly](/wiki/Roly_Poly_(Bob_Wills_song) "Roly Poly (Bob Wills song)")" with Asleep at the Wheel from the Country Music Association.[[11]](#cite_note-Front_Page-11) That same year, the band toured with [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") and [George Strait](/wiki/George_Strait "George Strait"). Benson recorded a tribute to Wills and Texas swing music, including Dwight Yoakam, [Vince Gill](/wiki/Vince_Gill "Vince Gill"), Merle Haggard, the Dixie Chicks, and Willie Nelson.[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4)[[12]](#cite_note-NPR-12) They were scheduled to play at the White House on the fateful September 11, 2001.[[4]](#cite_note-Official_Web_Site-4) In 2003, the band released *Live at [Billy Bob's Texas](/wiki/Billy_Bob%27s_Texas "Billy Bob's Texas")*, and by the end of the decade, the band had released two more albums: *Reinventing the Wheel*, an entirely new set of songs, including a collaboration with [The Blind Boys of Alabama](/wiki/The_Blind_Boys_of_Alabama "The Blind Boys of Alabama"), and a two-disc set *Kings of Texas Swing*.[[13]](#cite_note-Barnes_and_Noble-13) In 2007, their second Christmas album was released, *Santa Loves to Boogie*.

Asleep at the Wheel received six Austin Music Awards for their efforts in 2007, including Band of the Year, Songwriter of the Year (Benson), Country Band of the Year, Record Producer of the Year (Benson), Male Vocals of the Year (Benson), and Acoustic Guitar Player of the Year (McQueen).[[14]](#cite_note-Austin_Music_Awards-14)

In 2023, Katie Shore (vocals/fiddle) left the band for a solo career, but she still continues to sit in with the group on a semiregular basis.[[15]](#cite_note-Katie_Shore_leaves_2023-15)

## Members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asleep_at_the_Wheel&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Members")]

Main article: [List of Asleep at the Wheel members](/wiki/List_of_Asleep_at_the_Wheel_members "List of Asleep at the Wheel members")

**Current members**

* [Ray Benson](/wiki/Ray_Benson "Ray Benson") – lead vocals, lead guitar (1970–present)
* Ian Stewart - Fiddle, vocals (Late 2024 - present)
* Dennis Ludiker – fiddle, mandolin, backing vocals (2016–present)
* Connor Forsyth – piano, organ, accordion, backing vocals (2016–present)
* Josh Hoag – bass (2017–present)
* Curtis Clogston – steel guitars (2020–present)
* Joey Colarusso – saxophone (2021–present)
* Russ Patterson - drums (2023–present)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asleep_at_the_Wheel&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Discography")]

Main article: [Asleep at the Wheel discography](/wiki/Asleep_at_the_Wheel_discography "Asleep at the Wheel discography")

* *[Framed](/wiki/Framed_(Asleep_at_the_Wheel_album) "Framed (Asleep at the Wheel album)")* (1980)
* *[Pasture Prime](/wiki/Pasture_Prime "Pasture Prime")* (released as Asleep at the Wheel in the US) (1985)
* *[10](/wiki/10_(Asleep_at_the_Wheel_album) "10 (Asleep at the Wheel album)")* (1987)
* *[Western Standard Time](/wiki/Western_Standard_Time_(album) "Western Standard Time (album)")* (1988)
* *[Keepin' Me Up Nights](/wiki/Keepin%27_Me_Up_Nights "Keepin' Me Up Nights")* (1990)
* *[Tribute to the Music of Bob Wills and the Texas Playboys](/wiki/Tribute_to_the_Music_of_Bob_Wills_and_the_Texas_Playboys "Tribute to the Music of Bob Wills and the Texas Playboys")* (1993)
* *[The Wheel Keeps on Rollin'](/wiki/The_Wheel_Keeps_on_Rollin%27 "The Wheel Keeps on Rollin'")* (1995)
* *[Merry Texas Christmas, Y'all](/wiki/Merry_Texas_Christmas,_Y%27all "Merry Texas Christmas, Y'all")* (1997)
* *[Ride with Bob: A Tribute to Bob Wills and the Texas Playboys](/wiki/Ride_with_Bob:_A_Tribute_to_Bob_Wills_and_the_Texas_Playboys "Ride with Bob: A Tribute to Bob Wills and the Texas Playboys")* (1999)
* *[The Very Best of Asleep at the Wheel](/wiki/The_Very_Best_of_Asleep_at_the_Wheel "The Very Best of Asleep at the Wheel")* (2001)
* *[Hang Up My Spurs](/wiki/Hang_Up_My_Spurs "Hang Up My Spurs")* (2002)
* *[Asleep at the Wheel Remembers the Alamo](/wiki/Asleep_at_the_Wheel_Remembers_the_Alamo "Asleep at the Wheel Remembers the Alamo")* (2003)
* *[Reinventing the Wheel](/wiki/Reinventing_the_Wheel_(album) "Reinventing the Wheel (album)")* (2007)
* *Santa Loves to Boogie* (2007)
* *[Willie and the Wheel](/wiki/Willie_and_the_Wheel "Willie and the Wheel")* (2009) (with [Willie Nelson](/wiki/Willie_Nelson "Willie Nelson"))
* *It's a Good Day* (2010) (with [Leon Rausch](/wiki/Leon_Rausch "Leon Rausch"))
* *[Still the King: Celebrating the Music of Bob Wills and His Texas Playboys](/wiki/Still_the_King_(album) "Still the King (album)")* (2015)
* *Lone Star Christmas Night* (2016)
* *[New Routes](/wiki/New_Routes_(Asleep_at_the_Wheel_album) "New Routes (Asleep at the Wheel album)")* (2018)
* *[Half a Hundred Years](/wiki/Half_a_Hundred_Years "Half a Hundred Years")* (2021)

## Awards and nominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asleep_at_the_Wheel&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Awards and nominations")]

Asleep at the Wheel has won eight [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") out of 27 nominations.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The band has also been nominated for 12 [Country Music Association Awards](/wiki/Country_Music_Association_Awards "Country Music Association Awards").[[17]](#cite_note-17) In 1977, they won the [Academy of Country Music Award](/wiki/Academy_of_Country_Music_Award "Academy of Country Music Award") for Touring Band of the Year and have been nominated an additional nine times.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
